rationale: The citing paper discusses Morales and Fitzsimons (2007) in detail in the conceptual background, summarizing its core finding that product-to-product contact with disgusting items lowers evaluations and is driven by disgust. It then uses that result as key prior evidence that retail contagion effects had been documented mainly as negative, which motivates its own hypotheses about when contagion can turn positive. The cited study is also revisited in the discussion to contrast prior null evidence for positive product contagion with the new positive person-to-product findings.
relation_description: The citing paper extends Morales and Fitzsimons (2007) by moving from negative product-to-product contagion to conditions under which contagion becomes positive in a retail setting when the contact source is an attractive opposite-sex person. The cited paper provides the immediate empirical baseline that the new contribution builds on.
